candidate.question_text: What is a univariate time series?
source_support:
Univariate plots are designed to learn as much as possible about the
distribution, central tendency and spread of the data at hand. In
this chapter you will be presented with some visual tools used to
diagnose univariate times series.

candidate.question_text: What can a univariate time series plot tell me about distribution, central tendency, and spread?
candidate.rationale: Interpretive procedure question grounded in the section summary.
source_support:

candidate.question_text: How would I know when a univariate view is not enough for the question I want to answer?
candidate.rationale: Diagnostic question about the limits of a single-series view.
source_support:

candidate.question_text: What is the difference between a univariate and a multivariate time series?
candidate.rationale: Comparison question anchored in the section contrast.
source_support:
What to do if you have to deal with multivariate time series? In
this chapter, you will learn how to identify patterns in the
distribution, central tendency and spread over pairs or groups of
data.

candidate.question_text: How do I decide whether a candidate stock improves my existing portfolio?
candidate.rationale: Decision question grounded in the case study.
source_support:
Let's put everything you learned so far in practice! Imagine you
already own a portfolio of stocks and you have some spare cash to
invest, how can you wisely select a new stock to invest your
additional cash? Analyzing the statistical properties of individual
stocks vs. an existing portfolio is a good way of approaching the
problem.

candidate.question_text: Why is looking at one stock by itself not enough in the portfolio case study?

candidate.question_text: What would count as evidence that a stock complements my current portfolio rather than just looking strong on its own?
candidate.rationale: Transfer question about applying the case-study reasoning to a decision.
